name: Sammy Nestico
abstract: Samuel Louis Nestico (born Samuel Luigi Nistico; February 6, 1924 – January 17, 2021) was an American jazz composer and arranger. Nestico is best known for his arrangements for the Count Basie orchestra.

about_quotes.speaker: Marty Morell
about_quotes.evidence: I did a lot of dates and sessions with Sammy. ... he always asked for me in the rhythm section
about_quotes.doc_title: Marty Morell interviewed by Monk Rowe, Orlando, Florida, February 24, 2017

about_quotes.speaker: Charles Calello
about_quotes.evidence: I split the date with Sammy Nestico, which was a thrill for me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Charles Calello interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, July 21, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Foster
about_quotes.evidence: Sammy Nestico had already established himself as the Basie writer of the ’70s and ’80s. I didn’t have a full appreciation of that... I’m looking inside at him and I’m thinking, that’s great stuff, but that’s not my idea of what would be typical Basie.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Foster — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Wayne Bergeron
about_quotes.evidence: I do a lot of other things to make a living but my wife will tell you when I’m playing, you know, a Billy Byers’ chart, or a Frank Foster chart, or a Sammy Nestico chart, I just go Pffw — here we go.
about_quotes.doc_title: Wayne Bergeron interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, December 29, 2020

about_quotes.speaker: Butch Miles
about_quotes.evidence: Sammy Nestico is just one of my all time favorite Basie writers. He’s one of my all time favorite writers anyway, he’s a great, great arranger, just marvelous.
about_quotes.doc_title: Butch Miles interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, Scottsdale, Arizona, March 4, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Byron Stripling
about_quotes.evidence: He had no reason to be studying old Sammy Nestico charts that we were playing a lot of. He conducted all of them. He didn’t know those charts.
about_quotes.doc_title: Byron Stripling interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 25, 1998 [interview 1 of 2]
